query: role of interleukin-4 in antibody class switching in asthma

retrieved_docs.title: Dupilumab -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: Dupilumab is a fully human monoclonal antibody of the immunoglobulin G4 subclass designed to inhibit receptor signaling downstream of the JAK-STAT pathway by blocking interleukin-4/interleukin-13 (IL-4/IL-13) receptors. [4] As an IL-4Rα antagonist, dupilumab inhibits the signaling of pro-inflammatory cytokines, or interleukins, that induce inflammatory and immunological responses in several conditions, including eczema, asthma, allergic reactions, and rhinosinusitis. [18] Essentially, IL-4Rα is a component common to both IL-4 and IL-13 receptor complexes and is ubiquitously expressed in innate and adaptive immune cells to promote the signaling of IL-4 and IL-13. [19] [20] By blocking this pathway, dupilumab affects 3 integral disease mechanisms of atopic dermatitis: the decrease of skin barrier function, the class switch to IgE, and the Th2 differentiation. [4] [21]

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Immunoglobulin E -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: The role of IgE is central in allergy sensitization and atopic disorders such as allergic rhinitis, asthma, and atopic dermatitis. These disorders manifest due to type I hypersensitivity reactions involving IgE and other immune cells to produce the clinical symptoms seen in those disorders ultimately. It starts with an initial exposure to an antigen or allergen, which is taken up and processed by a dendritic cell or macrophage, which presents the antigen to a T-cell. In the presence of cytokine mediators IL-4 and IL-13, these T-cells are induced to differentiate into TH2 helper T-cells capable of presenting the antigen to B-cells. [6] The B-cells then undergo class-switching recombination events to produce Immunoglobulin E antibodies capable of binding to the presented antigen at the "antigen binding site" of the Fab portion of the antibody. Once this initial sensitization to antigen has occurred, more immunological events can ensure a more robust IgE response. The CD23 receptor on intestinal or airway epithelial cells can transport antigen-IgE complexes across the epithelium to bind Fc-epsilon-RI receptors on mast cells, macrophages, and other immune cells to promote inflammation, cytokine production, and more local IgE production. Furthermore, via interaction with the CD23 receptor on B-cells and myeloid cells, a process known as FAP can occur.

retrieved_docs.title: Interleukin -- Function -- Interleukin-4 (IL-4)
retrieved_docs.content: CD4+T cells (Th2) synthesize IL-4, and it acts on both B and T cells. It is a B-cell growth factor and causes IgE and IgG1 isotype selection. It causes Th2 differentiation and proliferation, and it inhibits IFN gamma-mediated activation on macrophages. It promotes mast cell proliferation in vivo. [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Immune Response -- Mechanism -- Hypersensitivity Reactions
retrieved_docs.content: Type 1 hypersensitivity reactions: Initial exposure to the antigen stimulates Th2 cells, which release IL-4, leading the B cells to switch from producing IgM to IgE antibodies, which are antigen-specific. These IgE antibodies bind to mast cells and basophils, sensitizing them to the antigen. Upon subsequent exposure to the allergen, the bound IgE on sensitized mast cells and basophils is cross-linked, causing the degranulation and release of preformed mediators, including histamine, leukotrienes, and prostaglandins. These events cause system ic vasodilation, bronchoconstriction, and increased permeability of the vascular endothelium. The reaction can be divided into 2 stages as follows: Immediate response: The release of preformed mediators causes the immune response. Late-phase response: Occurs 8 to 12 hours later, when the cytokines released in the immediate stage stimulate basophils, eosinophils, and neutrophils  even though the allergen has been removed.

retrieved_docs.title: Type IV Hypersensitivity Reaction -- Pathophysiology -- Subdivisions of Type IV Hypersensitivity Reactions
retrieved_docs.content: Type IVb: Type IVb hypersensitivity reactions involve Th2 cells that secrete interleukins, such as IL-4, IL-13, and IL-5, promoting B-cell production of IgE and IgG4 while triggering an inflammatory response from eosinophils and mast cells. These reactions are likely associated with DRESS/DIHS and allergic asthma.

retrieved_docs.title: Asthma Medication in Children -- Introduction -- Medications, Biologics, and Immunotherapy for Pediatric Asthma
retrieved_docs.content: Anti-interleukin-4 receptor α monoclonal antibody: Dupilumab is an IgG4 antibody that inhibits IL-4 and IL-13 signaling by binding to the IL-4R α subunit, inhibiting the release of proinflammatory cytokines and chemokines. [24]

retrieved_docs.title: Type I Hypersensitivity Reaction -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Simultaneously, B cells internalize the allergen and process it through the major histocompatibility complex class II (MHC II) pathway. B cells present the antigen-MHC II complex to Th2 cells, which recognize the complex through their T-cell receptor. CD40 on the B cells also binds with CD40L on the Th2 cell surface, stimulating the release of IL-4 and IL-13 by the Th2 cell. Costimulatory interaction between CD40 on the B cell and CD40L on the Th2 cell is essential for B-cell activation and isotype switching from IgM to IgE. IL-4 and IL-13 further drive this class switching. The resulting allergen-specific IgE binds to high-affinity FcεRI receptors on the surface of mast cells and basophils. Upon reexposure, the antigen can cross-link the previously bound IgE on mast cells and basophils, triggering the release of a range of preformed and newly synthesized inflammatory mediators. These mediators include:

retrieved_docs.title: Asthma Medications -- Clinical Significance -- Monoclonal Antibody Immune-Modulating Drugs
retrieved_docs.content: Dupilumab, a human monoclonal antibody to the IL-4 receptor, blocks both IL-4 and IL-13 signaling. Wenzel et al. demonstrated a 60% to 80% reduction in exacerbations in asthma patients already on inhaled corticosteroid-LABA combination. The responders did have higher baseline IgE levels. It is yet to be FDA approved for asthma.

retrieved_docs.title: Atopy -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: IL-4 and IL-13 promote IgE switching, whereas IL-5 stimulates eosinophil growth and activation. β2-adrenergic receptors regulate the contraction of bronchial smooth muscles. Chromosome 6p contains genes that code for major histocompatibility complex (MHC) class II proteins, and some of these alleles regulate T-cell responses to environmental antigens or allergens. A gene in chromosome 11q, which codes for the high-affinity IgE receptor β-subunit, mediates mast cell activation. Chromosome 12q houses genes that code for stem cell factor (affects mast cell growth and differentiation), interferon-γ (IFN-γ; inhibits IL-4 synthesis), and STAT6 (mediates IL-4 signal transduction).

retrieved_docs.title: Asthma -- Pathophysiology -- Airway Inflammation
retrieved_docs.content: The activation of mast cells by cytokines and other mediators plays a pivotal role in the development of clinical asthma. Following initial allergen inhalation, affected patients produce specific IgE antibodies due to an overexpression of the T-helper 2 subset (Th2) of lymphocytes relative to the Th1 type. Cytokines produced by Th2 lymphocytes include IL-4, IL-5, and IL-13, which promote IgE and eosinophilic responses in atopy. Once produced, these specific IgE antibodies bind to receptors on mast cells and basophils. Upon additional allergen inhalation, allergen-specific IgE antibodies on the mast cell surface undergo cross-linking, leading to rapid degranulation and the release of histamine, prostaglandin D2 (PGD2), and cysteinyl leukotrienes C4 (LTC4), D4 (LTD4), and E4 (LTE4). [23] [24] This triggers contraction of the airway smooth muscle within minutes and may stimulate reflex neural pathways. Subsequently, an influx of inflammatory cells, including monocytes, dendritic cells, neutrophils, T lymphocytes, eosinophils, and basophils, may lead to delayed bronchoconstriction several hours later.

retrieved_docs.title: Interleukin -- Function -- Interleukin-13 (IL-13)
retrieved_docs.content: CD4+T cells (Th2), NKT cells and mast cells synthesize IL-13. It acts on monocytes, fibroblasts, epithelial cells and B cells. The IL-13 significant effects are B-cell growth and differentiation, stimulates isotype switching to IgE. It causes increased mucus production by epithelial cells, increased collagen synthesis by fibroblasts and inhibits pro-inflammatory cytokine production. Also, IL-13 works together with IL-4 in producing biologic effects associated with allergic inflammation and in defense against parasites. [17]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Active Immunity -- Mechanism
retrieved_docs.content: If IL-12 and IFN-gamma signals are not present, TH0 cells become TH2 cells. The TH2 cells are activated by dendritic cells presenting MHC II-antigen complexes on their surface. Once activated, TH2 cells release IL-4, IL-5, IL-6, and IL-10 cytokines, which stimulate a humoral (antibody) response. These cytokines promote B cell immunoglobulin class switching from IgM and IgD to IgG, IgE, and IgA. Antibodies play a role in eliminating infectious pathogens, preventing the hematologic spread, and providing future protection if re-exposed.

retrieved_docs.title: Pathophysiology Of Asthma -- Mechanism
retrieved_docs.content: There are two phases of an asthma exacerbation, which include the early phase and the late phase. The early phase is initiated by IgE antibodies that are sensitized and released by plasma cells. These antibodies respond to certain triggers in the environment, such as the risk factors listed above. IgE antibodies then bind to high-affinity mast cells and basophils. When a pollutant or risk factor gets inhaled, the mast cells release cytokines and eventually de-granulate. Released from mast cells are histamine, prostaglandins, and leukotrienes. These cells, in turn, contract the smooth muscle and cause airway tightening. [12] Th2 lymphocytes play an integral role where they produce a series of interleukins (IL-4, IL-5, IL-13) and GM-CSF, which aid in communication with other cells and sustain inflammation. IL-3 and IL-5 help eosinophils and basophils survive. IL-13 is involved in remodeling, fibrosis, and hyperplasia. [13] Within the next several hours, the late phase occurs, in which eosinophils, basophils, neutrophils, and helper and memory T-cells all localize to the lungs as well, which perform bronchoconstriction and cause inflammation. Mast cells also play an essential role in bringing the late-phase reactants to the inflamed sites. [14] It is critical to recognize both of these two mechanisms to target therapy and relieve both bronchoconstriction and inflammation, depending on the severity of the disease. Interestingly, those with a thicker airway over time have a longer disease duration due to a narrower airway. [15] As a result of inflammation and bronchoconstriction, there is an intermittent airflow obstruction, resulting in increased work of breathing.

retrieved_docs.title: Asthma Medications -- Function
retrieved_docs.content: Asthma is classically recognized as a typical Th2 disease, with increased IgE levels and eosinophilic inflammation in the airway. IL-4 and IL-13 trigger IgE production. IL-4 and IL-9 trigger mast cell activity. IL-3 triggers basophil activity. IL-5 is the primary trigger for eosinophil activity along with some input from IL-3 and GM-CSF. All components listed above are involved in the inflammatory response which leads to degranulation of histamine, prostaglandins, leukotrienes, among other enzymes. This induces bronchial hyper-responsiveness, increased mucous secretion, and ultimately airway obstruction and remodeling that is classic to asthma. Key points in this cascade that are currently susceptible to modulation include IgE and IL-5, while medications using IL-4 and IL-13 as targets are in clinical trials.

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Immunoglobulin A -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: IgA gets produced by class switching of Ig, which is regulated by various processes. The binding of CD40-CD40L and secretion of other cytokines IL-4, IL-5, IL-6, IL-10, and IL-21 promote maturation of Th2 cells, which promote class switching to different Ig subtypes. Retinoic acid, a metabolite of vitamin A, synergistically acts with IL-5 and IL-6 to induce IgA secretion as well. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Asthma Medications -- Clinical Significance -- Monoclonal Antibody Immune-Modulating Drugs
retrieved_docs.content: Newer class monoclonal antibody immune-modulating drugs can be used in specific situations where patients do not respond effectively to inhaled glucocorticoid therapy and are found to have eosinophilia. These medications aim to decouple the Th2 inflammatory pathway to decrease the immune response to a triggering event, effectively decreasing eosinophilia.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 4 -- Community 2792
retrieved_docs.content: Methapyrilen and omalizumab are both assigned to the therapeutic class of substances that affect the respiratory system. As members of this class, they are used to influence breathing function and are relevant in the treatment of respiratory disorders. Omalizumab, an anti‑IgE monoclonal antibody, is employed in the management of allergic asthma and other IgE‑mediated respiratory conditions, while methapyrilen, a respiratory‑acting agent, is utilized to modify airway dynamics. Recognizing that both drugs belong to the same therapeutic category helps clinicians consider them when selecting pharmacologic strategies aimed at modulating respiratory function.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 2 -- Community 400
retrieved_docs.content: Benralizumab, mepolizumab, reslizumab, roflumilast, and tezepirumab are all classified as members of the ATC group “other systemic drugs for obstructive airway diseases.” This category groups systemic therapies that are used to treat obstructive airway conditions such as asthma and chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD). The biologic agents benralizumab, mepolizumab, reslizumab, and tezepirumab target specific inflammatory pathways (e.g., interleukin‑5 or thymic stromal lymphopoietin) to reduce airway inflammation, while roflumilast is a phosphodiesterase‑4 inhibitor that modulates inflammatory cell activity. Recognizing that these diverse agents belong to the same ATC class helps clinicians understand their shared therapeutic purpose and facilitates systematic prescribing, pharmacovigilance, and comparative effectiveness assessments in the management of obstructive airway diseases.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 2 -- Community 697
retrieved_docs.content: Amlexanox, benralizumab, mepolizumab, reslizumab, roflumilast, and tezepelumab are all classified as members of the “other systemic drugs for obstructive airway diseases” category in the Anatomical Therapeutic Chemical (ATC) system. This grouping indicates that each of these agents is used systemically to treat obstructive airway conditions such as asthma and chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD). The list includes several monoclonal antibodies—benralizumab, mepolizumab, reslizumab, and tezepelumab—that target specific inflammatory pathways (e.g., interleukin‑5 or thymic stromal lymphopoietin) to reduce airway inflammation, as well as roflumilast, a phosphodiesterase‑4 inhibitor that helps control inflammation and bronchoconstriction. Amlexanox, although less commonly used for airway disease, is also placed in this category, reflecting its systemic therapeutic role. Grouping these diverse agents together highlights the range of systemic pharmacologic strategies available for managing obstructive airway diseases and underscores their shared classification despite differing mechanisms of action.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 3 -- Community 1884
retrieved_docs.content: Amlexanox, mepolizumab, reslizumab and tezepelumab are all classified as members of the ATC group “other systemic drugs for obstructive airway diseases.” This categorization indicates that each of these agents is a systemic therapy used to treat obstructive airway conditions such as asthma or chronic obstructive pulmonary disease. Although they share the same therapeutic class, the drugs differ in their mechanisms of action: amlexanox is a small‑molecule agent, mepolizumab and reslizumab are monoclonal antibodies that target interleukin‑5, and tezepelumab is a monoclonal antibody directed against thymic stromal lymphopoietin. Their inclusion in the same ATC subgroup underscores their common role in managing severe or refractory obstructive airway disease, while highlighting the variety of pharmacologic strategies available within this therapeutic category.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 4 -- Community 2718
retrieved_docs.content: Amlexanox, benralizumab, mepolizumab and reslizumab are all classified within the Anatomical Therapeutic Chemical (ATC) group “other systemic drugs for obstructive airway diseases.” This ATC category comprises systemic agents that are used to manage obstructive airway conditions such as asthma and chronic obstructive pulmonary disease. By being members of the same pharmacological class, these drugs share a therapeutic purpose of improving airway function, although they achieve this through different mechanisms—benralizumab and mepolizumab are monoclonal antibodies that target the interleukin‑5 pathway, reslizumab also acts on the IL‑5 axis, and amlexanox exerts anti‑inflammatory effects. Recognizing that all four agents belong to this specific ATC group helps clinicians understand their role in the broader treatment strategy for obstructive airway diseases and facilitates appropriate drug selection and therapeutic planning.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 1 -- Community 116
retrieved_docs.content: Amlexanox is classified as a member of the ATC group “other systemic drugs for obstructive airway diseases,” indicating its systemic use in the management of airway obstruction. Within the overarching therapeutic category “DRUGS FOR OBSTRUCTIVE AIRWAY DISEASES,” which encompasses both systemic and inhaled agents, the subclass “other systemic drugs for obstructive airway diseases in ATC” is a child of this broad class and is linked to the larger adrenergics‑for‑systemic‑use group, reflecting that many of its members act on adrenergic pathways. Systemic agents in this subclass include fenspiride, benralizumab, mepolizumab, reslizumab, roflumilast and tezepelumab, representing mechanisms such as non‑selective β‑adrenergic activity (fenspiride), IL‑5‑targeting monoclonal antibodies (benralizumab, mepolizumab, reslizumab), phosphodiesterase‑4 inhibition (roflumilast) and thymic stromal lymphopoietin blockade (tezepelumab) for asthma and related obstructive diseases. The inhaled branch is

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 4 -- Community 2728
retrieved_docs.content: Benralizumab and mepolizumab are both classified as members of the ATC category “other systemic drugs for obstructive airway diseases,” indicating that each of these agents is recognized as a systemic medication used in the treatment of obstructive airway conditions such as asthma. This classification groups the two drugs together within the same therapeutic class, highlighting their shared role in managing airway inflammation and improving respiratory outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 2 -- Community 690
retrieved_docs.content: Fenspiride, benralizumab, mepolizumab, reslizumab and tezepelumab are all classified within the Anatomical Therapeutic Chemical (ATC) group “other systemic drugs for obstructive airway diseases.” This categorisation indicates that each of these agents is used as a systemic therapy for conditions characterized by airway obstruction, such as asthma or chronic obstructive pulmonary disease, when inhaled treatments are insufficient. Although they share a common therapeutic class, the drugs have distinct mechanisms of action: fenspiride is a non‑steroidal anti‑inflammatory and bronchodilator; benralizumab, mepolizumab and reslizumab are monoclonal antibodies that target the interleukin‑5 pathway to reduce eosinophilic inflammation; and tezepelumab is a monoclonal antibody that blocks thymic stromal lymphopoietin (TSLP), a key upstream cytokine in airway inflammation. Their inclusion in the same ATC subgroup underscores their role as systemic options for managing obstructive airway diseases, guiding clinicians in selecting appropriate non‑inhaled treatments for patients with severe or refractory disease.

By belonging to the same ATC class, these three drugs share a therapeutic focus on modulating inflammatory pathways or other mechanisms to improve airway function, highlighting their related roles in the management of obstructive airway disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 1446
retrieved_docs.content: Amlexanox, benralizumab, mepolizumab, reslizumab, and tezepelumab are all classified as members of the ATC group “other systemic drugs for obstructive airway diseases.” This category groups systemic therapies that are used to treat obstructive airway conditions such as asthma, distinguishing them from inhaled bronchodilators and corticosteroids. By sharing this classification, these agents are recognized as systemic options that target specific inflammatory pathways in severe or refractory disease, helping clinicians select appropriate non‑inhaled treatments for patients with obstructive airway disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 4 -- Community 2708
retrieved_docs.content: Albuterol and omalizumab are both members of the therapeutic class described as “látky ovlivňující dýchací systém” (substances that affect the respiratory system). This classification indicates that each drug exerts its primary pharmacologic action on the respiratory tract: albuterol functions as a bronchodilator commonly used to relieve bronchospasm in asthma and chronic obstructive pulmonary disease, while omalizumab is an anti‑IgE monoclonal antibody employed to control severe allergic asthma and other IgE‑mediated respiratory conditions. Recognizing that both agents belong to the same respiratory‑system‑targeting therapeutic class helps clinicians understand their related mechanisms and consider them within a unified framework for managing airway diseases.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 3 -- Community 1727
retrieved_docs.content: Thyreoglobulin and omalizumab are recognized as permissible qualifiers in the context of ultrastructure, meaning that both of these agents may be used to label or characterize ultrastructural features in microscopic examinations. This allowance indicates that researchers and clinicians can employ thyreoglobulin, a thyroid hormone precursor protein, and omalizumab, an anti‑IgE monoclonal antibody, as specific markers when assessing cellular and subcellular architecture, thereby enhancing the precision of ultrastructural analysis.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 4 -- Community 2923
retrieved_docs.content: Anti‑asthmatic therapy involves the corticosteroid beclomethasone, which is classified as an inhaled steroid agent (ISA) and is used to manage airway disease. Beclomethasone specifically targets inflammation (الالتهاب), reducing inflammatory processes in the respiratory tract and thereby improving asthma control. This highlights beclomethasone’s central role as an anti‑asthmatic medication that treats inflammatory airway conditions.
